narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, LSAT score of 30, upper-middle family income, full-time student status, and notably low Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 2nd and 1st respectively—and predicted an 88.2% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk directly led to the rejection of your admission, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed on the bar to maintain strong outcomes. The most influential factor pushing the model toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your school that year—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing—and signaled potential struggles with sustaining strong performance. Next in importance, your Year 1 decile of 2nd similarly pulled the prediction toward failure, as it highlighted early academic challenges compared to the average applicant's 6th decile position. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which exceeds the typical 3.21 average, worked against the failure prediction by demonstrating solid prior college achievement. While your LSAT score of 30 is below the dataset average of 36.7, and other details like your full-time status and family income played smaller roles, it was primarily those low decile rankings that dominated the decision. In comparison to typical students who pass the bar, your early and later school rankings stand out as unusually weak, outweighing strengths like your GPA and overriding the commonality of being a full-time applicant like 93% of others. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male student with upper-middle resources—many in similar demographics succeed with stronger consistent academics—but the model saw a clear risk pattern here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your recent school performance through additional coursework or tutoring to improve future decile rankings, and reapplying next cycle with updated credentials that address these key gaps. Schools value persistence, and targeted improvements in these areas could shift your predicted odds dramatically in your favor.
